hook: A prehistoric cave under the cactus garden, where Ice Age hunters sheltered.
facts: Discovered in 1916 during excavation for the Jardin Exotique foundations.
Descent is via 300+ steps to about 40 m above sea level; limestone stalactites, stalagmites, draperies and columns throughout.
Visited in prehistory by hunter-gatherers; animal bones and tools were excavated and rescue digs were led by Canon Leonce de Villeneuve.
Open to the public since 1950.
w: In 1916, garden workers breaking ground for the cactus terraces above broke instead into a cavern of stalactites and Ice Age bones. Three hundred steps drop you 40 metres into it, where Neanderthals once sheltered. Monaco's oldest address, hidden beneath its newest garden.

hook: An 18th-century fortress, blown up in 1944 and reborn as a cliffside open-air theatre.
facts: Built in the early 18th century during the War of the Spanish Succession.
Blown up by retreating German troops on 3 September 1944.
Rebuilt under Prince Rainier III in 1953 and used as an open-air theatre since 1970.
Seats about 350 and retains cannons and a central pyramid of cannonballs.
w: Built to guard the Rock during the War of the Spanish Succession, this horseshoe bastion was dynamited by retreating Germans in 1944. Rainier III rebuilt it in 1953, and since 1970 actors have trod its stones each summer. Cannonballs still pyramid by the stage. Gunfire to greasepaint.

hook: Monaco's oldest public gardens, cut into the south face of the Rock.
facts: Created in 1816 under Prince Honore V, the first public gardens in Monaco, partly as famine-relief work.
Mediterranean planting of holm oaks, myrtle and pistachio, later enriched with exotics.
Cliffside paths lead past small caves that shelter bats.
A bronze statue of Prince Albert I as a navigator stands among the paths.
w: Monaco's first public garden, carved into the cliff in 1816 under Honore V partly to give the hungry work. Holm oak and myrtle shade winding paths above the sea, past caves where bats roost and a bronze Albert I stands at the helm of an imaginary ship. The Rock's green edge.

hook: A pin-sharp Zen garden by the sea, blessed by a Shinto priest.
facts: Created in 1994 at the request of Prince Rainier III and designed by landscape architect Yasuo Beppu.
Covers about 7,000 square metres with a koi pond, waterfall, stone lanterns and a teahouse.
The site was blessed in 1992 by a Shinto high priest from Dazaifu in Fukuoka.
Timber, lanterns and tiles were imported from Japan; the planting is mostly Mediterranean.
w: Rainier III wanted a true Zen garden by the sea, so a Shinto priest blessed the ground in 1992 and Osaka master Yasuo Beppu laid it out. Koi glide under arched bridges; gravel lies raked into still waves. Lanterns and timber shipped from Japan, set among Mediterranean pines.

hook: The locals' covered market, frying socca and barbagiuans since 1880.
facts: Established on 18 November 1880 under Mayor Count Felix Gastaldi.
Sits on Place d'Armes at the foot of the Rock, combining a covered hall, outdoor stalls and a modern gourmet hall.
About 20 traders including a fishmonger, butchers, bakers and delis.
Monegasque specialities served on the spot: socca, barbagiuans, pissaladiere and fougasse.
w: Since 1880 the locals have shopped here, under a high roof on the Place d'Armes while the Rock looms over the stalls. Skip the glamour: come for socca crisping on hot griddles, barbagiuans frying golden, and pissaladiere by the slice. Monaco with its apron on, far from the Casino.

hook: A concrete sun-deck on the seawall where swimmers ladder into deep water.
facts: Not a sand beach but an artificial two-level concrete structure on the Digue Rainier III, about 140 m long.
Terraced concrete steps and metal ladders give direct access to deep water, for confident swimmers and divers.
Amenities include bars, showers and changing cabins; attendance stays low even in summer.
Protects the south side of Port Hercule.
w: No sand here, just a long concrete sun-deck bolted to the great sea wall, where Monegasques climb down iron ladders straight into deep blue water. Divers and swimmers have it largely to themselves even in August. Part lido, part grandstand. Bring nerve, not a bucket and spade.

hook: James Bond himself rests in a modest hillside cemetery with a millionaire's view.
facts: Roger Moore, the James Bond actor, died on 23 May 2017 aged 89 and was buried in the Cimetiere de Monaco on 10 June 2017.
He had long ties to the Principality.
A 2024 report of his grave being vandalised was debunked: the family crest had simply come unstuck through weathering.
The cemetery sits on the western heights in the Jardin Exotique ward.
w: James Bond lies up on the western heights, in a hillside cemetery with a view most of the living would kill for. Roger Moore, knighted and beloved, was buried here in 2017 among Monaco's quieter residents. A 2024 vandalism scare proved false: only weather had loosened his crest. Shaken, not stirred.

hook: A landscaped park strung with Botero bronzes and a duck-filled pond.
facts: Landscaped park on reclaimed land, very popular with families.
The pond, renovated in 2020 with a wheelchair-accessible promenade, is home to ducks, turtles and fish.
The Chemin des Sculptures Rainier III runs through it with works by Arman, Botero, Calder and Cesar.
Adjoins the Princess Grace Rose Garden.
w: On land won back from the sea, a soft green park hides a surprising trail of bronzes by Botero, Calder, Arman and Cesar, scattered among the lawns. A pond renovated in 2020 paddles with ducks and turtles. Sculpture trail and family picnic, side by side beneath the towers.

hook: A century-old olive grove turned family park, still pressed for its own oil.
facts: Origins date to 1917, when Prince Albert I protected the area's olive groves from industrialisation.
Named for Prince Rainier III's sister, Princess Antoinette.
Century-old olive trees on the site are still pressed into oil.
Family draws include an 18-hole mini-golf, a mini-farm with goats, ducks and rabbits, and a playground.
w: Prince Albert I saved these olive groves from the builders in 1917, and a century on the old trees still yield their oil. Named for Rainier's sister, the park trades glamour for mini-golf, a petting farm of goats and ducks, and shade. Working olives in the world's priciest square mile.

hook: A pocket of tropical 'Little Africa' at the foot of the Casino.
facts: Tropical garden below the Casino de Monte-Carlo, planted with species such as Ficus macrophylla and Ginkgo biloba.
Created under Prince Charles III from 1893 to evoke an eternal spring of tropical flora.
Winding paths with fountains and sculptures frame views of the Place du Casino and the sea.
Sits beside the more formal Jardins des Boulingrins.
w: Below the Casino's marble steps spreads a slice of the tropics, planted from 1893 under Charles III to prove Monaco could grow an eternal spring. Giant figs and ginkgos lean over fountains and paths. Gamblers stride past; few drop into this cool green pocket. Little Africa, underfoot.

hook: Contemporary art in a Belle Epoque seaside villa once owned by a painter.
facts: One of two venues of the Nouveau Musee National de Monaco, the principality's contemporary-art museum.
The villa was owned by painter Robert Sauber from 1904 and listed as state heritage in 1969.
Offers about 650 square metres of exhibition space.
Hosts roughly two exhibitions a year; past artists include Tom Wesselmann and Gilbert and George.
w: A Belle Epoque villa by the sea, once home to the painter Robert Sauber, now shows cutting-edge art across 650 square metres of salon and garden. One of two homes for Monaco's contemporary museum, it swaps gilt for installation. Wesselmann and Gilbert and George have hung where a society painter lived.

hook: The museum's second villa, all Art Nouveau staircase and stained glass.
facts: The second venue of the Nouveau Musee National de Monaco, built in 1913.
Features an Art Nouveau staircase and stained glass by Fassi Cadet.
Acquired in 1995 and opened as an exhibition venue in 2010.
Offers about 875 square metres of space; has shown Joan Miro among others.
w: The museum's second house climbs a hill above town, a 1913 villa wrapped around an Art Nouveau staircase lit by Fassi Cadet's stained glass. Acquired in 1995, opened to art in 2010, its bright rooms have shown Miro and more. Half the Nouveau Musee, and the prettier stairwell.

hook: Summer films on a giant screen at the foot of the Rock, since 1948.
facts: Seasonal open-air cinema running since 1948, atop the Parking des Pecheurs on Avenue de la Quarantaine.
Claims Europe's largest open-air screen at about 220 square metres, with capacity around 500.
Films are shown in their original language with French subtitles.
Operates only in summer.
w: Since 1948, summer nights at the foot of the Rock have meant films on what may be Europe's largest open-air screen, 220 square metres glowing over 500 deckchairs. Original-language pictures, Mediterranean breeze, the cliff at your back. Cinema the old way, under actual stars.

hook: Renaissance frescoes of Hercules, hidden for a century under later paint.
facts: Sixteenth-century Italian Renaissance frescoes of the Labours of Hercules in the Galerie d'Hercule of the Prince's Palace.
Overpainted in the 19th century and again in 1962, then rediscovered from 2015.
Restoration was carried out under the patronage of Prince Albert II.
The State Apartments hold about 600 square metres of frescoes, including Ulysses and Europa cycles.
w: For a century the Prince's grand gallery wore dull 19th-century paint. Strip it back, restorers found from 2015, and 16th-century frescoes of Hercules and his labours blaze out beneath. Six hundred square metres of Renaissance myth, hidden in plain sight and freshly unveiled. Worth looking up.

hook: Six thousand roses laid out, from the air, in the shape of a single bloom.
facts: Inaugurated in 1984 by Prince Rainier III as a tribute to Princess Grace, two years after her death.
Holds more than 450 varieties and nearly 6,000 rose plants in concentric circles around a fountain.
From above, the garden's layout forms the silhouette of a blooming rose.
Certified Ecocert, having used no synthetic chemicals for over 15 years.
w: Rainier III planted 6,000 roses here in 1984, two years after losing Grace, and laid them in rings that from the air trace the shape of a single bloom. Four hundred varieties scent the Fontvieille air, grown without a chemical for decades. A widower's quiet, fragrant grief.

hook: Genoese walls and stacked cannonballs guarding the Grimaldi stronghold.
facts: The ramparts of the Rock were begun in 1215 as a Genoese fortress of four towers linked by a curtain wall.
Reinforced in the 16th century under Spanish and French influence.
Stacked cannonballs and cannons line the west side of Palace Square, pointed seaward over Fontvieille.
The changing of the guard happens daily at 11:55 on the square.
w: The Grimaldis have held this clifftop since 1297, and their Genoese walls from 1215 still ring the summit. Along the west terrace, cannonballs sit pyramided beside old guns aimed out over Fontvieille and the sea. At 11:55 sharp the guard still changes below. Seven centuries, dug in.

hook: The chapel of Monaco's patron saint, washed ashore by a storm and a dove.
facts: Dedicated to Saint Devote, patron of Monaco and of the Grimaldi family.
A chapel stood on the site before 1070; the present church was inaugurated on 25 January 1871 under Prince Charles III.
Her relics are kept here and at Monaco Cathedral.
Each 26 January a boat is burned on the forecourt to mark the legend.
legend: Legend holds that Devote, a 3rd or 4th-century Corsican martyr, was placed in a boat that a dove guided through a storm to run aground on this spot.
w: Legend says a martyred Corsican girl was set adrift, and a dove steered her boat through a storm to ground on this shore. Monaco took Devote as its patron, and each January burns a boat on the chapel forecourt to remember. The present church dates to 1871; her relics rest within. Faith, by sea.

hook: Prince Albert I's museum of Ice Age tools and bones from the Riviera caves.
facts: Founded in 1902 by Prince Albert I.
Reached via 56 bis Boulevard du Jardin Exotique, between the Jardin Exotique and Villa Paloma.
Displays Palaeolithic tools, Ice Age fauna and human remains from regional caves including the Grimaldi caves and the Observatory Cave.
Open daily from 9:00 to 18:00.
w: Prince Albert I, oceanographer and bone-hunter, founded this museum in 1902 to house the Ice Age finds from the Riviera's caves. Flint tools, mammoth teeth and the skulls of the Grimaldi caves fill its cases, dug from the cliffs nearby. The deep human past, gathered by a prince.

hook: The Prince's own garage: a hundred cars from De Dion-Bouton to F1.
facts: Holds about 100 vehicles in a 3,500-square-metre space, from a 1903 De Dion-Bouton to a 2013 Lotus F1.
Includes Monaco Grand Prix and Monte-Carlo Rally cars and the Lexus from the 2011 royal wedding.
Reopened at its new Port Hercule site in July 2022 after leaving the Terrasses de Fontvieille.
Built from the personal collection of Prince Rainier III.
w: The late Rainier III loved a fine motor car, and his hundred-strong collection runs from a spidery 1903 De Dion-Bouton to a 2013 Lotus F1. Grand Prix winners, rally legends and the royal-wedding Lexus share the hall, moved to Port Hercule in 2022. A petrolhead's shrine in a pocket state.

n: Musee des Timbres et des Monnaies
hook: Philately with a princely twist: Monaco's stamps and coins back to 1640.
facts: Established by Sovereign Ordinance of Prince Rainier III in 1995 and opened in January 1996.
Holds the Prince's personal collection, including Monaco's first stamps from 1885 under Charles III.
Coins on show go back to the first Monegasque coinage of 1640 under Honore II.
Displays a stamp printing press and a 19th-century monetary balance.
w: Monaco printed its first stamps in 1885 and minted coins as far back as 1640, and the Prince's own hoard of both fills this small Fontvieille gallery. A printing press and an old money-balance sit among the rarities. Philately and numismatics, with a crown on top. Quietly engrossing.

hook: Thousands of cacti clinging to a cliff, with the whole principality below.
facts: Built into a cliff face and holding more than 1,000 species and 6,000 varieties of succulents and cacti.
Terraced walkways and viewpoints look out over the Rock, Port Hercule and the Rivieras.
Restructured and renovated during a major closure-and-refurbishment programme in the 2020s.
The Grotte de l'Observatoire and the prehistory museum sit within the same complex.
w: Thousands of cacti and succulents cling to a sheer cliff here, some towering two storeys, on terraces that hang over the whole principality. A 1916 dig for its footings broke into a prehistoric cave below. Reborn after a long refit, it pairs desert spikes with the bluest of views.

hook: The slowest, tightest corner in Formula 1, walkable when the racing stops.
facts: The slowest corner in Formula 1, taken at about 48 km/h, a near 180-degree bend needing full steering lock.
Part of the Circuit de Monaco since the inaugural 1929 Grand Prix.
Renamed over the decades from Station to Loews to Fairmont, tracking the adjacent hotel's branding.
It is public road on Avenue des Spelugues, walkable outside race week.
w: Formula 1's slowest, tightest corner doubles as an ordinary hotel access road the other 51 weeks a year. Drivers crawl it at 48 km/h on full lock; you can simply stroll down and stand where legends inch past. Once Station, then Loews, now Fairmont. Walk the most famous bend in racing.

hook: Monaco's deep-water harbour, a port the ancients credited to Hercules himself.
facts: Monaco's only deep-water port, used as a Greek and Roman trading port and refuge.
The modern port was completed in 1926, improved in the 1970s and re-fitted with floating pontoons by 2011.
Hosts the Monaco Yacht Show each September and forms part of the Grand Prix circuit.
The ancient colony Monoikos took its name from local veneration of Hercules.
legend: Legend, recorded by Greek and Roman writers, holds that Hercules passed through on his way from Spain to Italy and built the port and coastal road.
w: The ancients swore Hercules himself dug this harbour as he crossed from Spain to Italy, and named the place Monoikos for him. Greek and Roman ships once sheltered where superyachts now jostle each September for the Yacht Show. The modern moles date to 1926. Myth and money, side by side.

hook: Pastel lanes and vaulted passages where the Grimaldi story began.
facts: The oldest district of Monaco, perched about 60 m above the sea on a fortified promontory.
Cradle of the Grimaldi dynasty since 1297.
A maze of narrow, mostly pedestrian cobbled lanes with pastel facades, flowery balconies and staircases.
Vehicle traffic is restricted, giving it a quiet, medieval-village atmosphere distinct from Monte-Carlo.
w: Up on the Rock, away from the Casino's glitter, the old town keeps its medieval bones: pastel lanes barely a car wide, vaulted passages, balconies dripping geraniums. The Grimaldis have ruled from here since 1297. Wander the quiet cobbles where everyday Monaco still lives. Village in a city-state.
